mechanism: An experimental design manipulates disclosure conditions (reveal vs. withhold information) and measures judgments and inferences from observers. Observers evaluate an entity's trustworthiness and character based on observed disclosure behavior. The mechanism compares means or proportions of judgments across disclosure conditions to infer whether withholding information affects social evaluation and whether decision-makers accurately predict these effects.

An experimental design assigns entities to conditions manipulating stereotype functionality and introduces novel groups with fabricated stereotypes. Outcomes measure evaluation and resource allocation to stereotypical vs. counterstereotypical entities. Analysis identifies whether desire-for-certainty mediates negative evaluation of counterstereotypical entities by comparing outcomes across political orientation and manipulated-certainty conditions.

Experimental design independently manipulates environmental-context information and group membership. Outcomes measure inferences about behavioral characteristics and life-history strategies. Analysis tests whether inferences about ecologic strategy override race-based stereotypes by comparing outcome patterns across race/ecology conditions and identifying interaction or dominance effects.

Participants engage in an action with ethical valence and subsequently report memory clarity for that action at multiple time points. Analysis measures decline in memory clarity (vividity, specificity) over temporal intervals by comparing memory metrics between ethical and unethical actions and examining temporal decay slopes.

A behavioral intervention manipulates teacher mindset (empathic vs. punitive framing) using an online training module. Outcomes measure student suspension rates, student motivation, and teacher-student relationship quality. Analysis estimates causal effects of mindset manipulation on suspension-rate outcomes via randomized assignment to intervention and control conditions.
domain: educational psychology, teacher training, student discipline

mechanism: An economic game manipulates observability of an entity's decision process (transparent costs or decision time vs. opaque) and measures cooperative choices and observer judgments of trustworthiness. Analysis tests whether process observability mediates the relationship between uncalculating cooperation and reputation by comparing choice patterns and trustworthiness ratings across visibility conditions.

An allocation task assigns entities to fair or unfair procedures for resource distribution. Before and after allocation, outcomes measure recipient happiness and allocator predictions of happiness. Analysis tests whether allocators systematically overestimate the impact of procedural fairness on outcomes by comparing predicted vs. actual happiness and examining whether procedure salience shifts between pre- and post-allocation.

An experimental manipulation induces subjective social rank or economic status perception (high vs. low relative standing). Outcomes measure appetite preference (implicit or explicit) and food intake quantity. Analysis compares food-preference and consumption metrics across induced-status conditions and tests whether resource-scarcity perception mediates appetite via hypothesized behavioral adaptation mechanisms.
domain: health psychology, socioeconomic status, appetite regulation

mechanism: An informational manipulation exposes entities to inequality-related comparisons (past vs. future temporal frame vs. control). Outcomes measure beliefs about climate-related opportunity and support for policy interventions. Analysis compares outcome measures across frame conditions stratified by political ideology to test whether past-focus comparisons reduce conservative-liberal divergence in climate outcomes.
domain: environmental psychology, political psychology, climate change

mechanism: A direct-mail field experiment randomly assigns households to receive either conditional incentive gifts or control. Outcomes measure donation rate, donation amount, and attention allocation (implicitly). Analysis estimates causal effects of incentive presence on donation behavior by comparing donation metrics across conditions and uses attention-based mechanisms to explain crowding-out effects observed in high-prior-motivation subgroups.

Facial image features of scientists are rated on perceived traits (competence, morality, attractiveness) using human judgment or computational extraction. Outcomes measure lay-audience selection of science content and quality-judgment ratings. Analysis tests associations between face-derived traits and content selection/quality judgments via regression or correlation, establishing whether first-impression biases propagate to science-communication decisions.
domain: science communication, social psychology, visual perception

mechanism: Belief in free will (measured or experimentally manipulated) is related to the degree to which an entity attributes others' behavior to internal vs. external causes via regression or comparison. Subsequent analysis links belief-in-free-will and attribution patterns to prescribed punishment/reward behavior. Mediation analysis tests whether correspondence bias transmits the effect of free-will belief onto behavioral prescriptions.
domain: social psychology, attribution theory, free will

mechanism: A multi-method approach combines evolutionary game-theoretic analysis, reinforcement-learning simulation, and behavioral experiments. The mechanism represents a two-player strategic interaction (thief vs. victim) and models learning dynamics via RL or adaptive algorithms. Analysis identifies whether learning processes favor rigid or flexible punishment strategies by comparing evolutionary stability and behavioral data across learning regimes.
domain: evolutionary psychology, game theory, social punishment

mechanism: Perceived humanness (explicit ratings or implicit inference from dehumanizing language) is related to reported support for harm via survey or vignette experiment. Outcomes include endorsement of harmful practices (instrumental or moral) and willingness to harm for different motives. Analysis tests whether dehumanization predicts instrumental harm support but not moral harm support via logistic regression or comparison of odds-ratios across harm-motive types.

An informational manipulation exposes entities to factual inequality data (rising inequality vs. control information). Outcomes measure beliefs about structural vs. individual contributors to economic advantage and policy-support ratings. Analysis identifies shifts in opportunity beliefs and policy support via comparison of means across information conditions and subsequent mediation by opportunity skepticism.

Participants generate self-disclosures (photographs) under manipulated permanence conditions (temporary vs. permanent sharing platform). Outcomes measure compliance with disclosure request, disclosure-risk indicators, and observer impressions of discloser judgment. Analysis compares outcome metrics across permanence and observer-experience conditions to isolate whether permanence affects disclosure behavior but not observer evaluation, and identifies privacy concern as a mediator.

Participants make intertemporal choice (present vs. future reward) while their information-search patterns are recorded and classified (comparative vs. integrative strategies). Outcomes measure discount rates and choice patterns under framing manipulation. Analysis identifies heterogeneous search strategies via clustering or classification and tests whether search-strategy type causally affects discounting via experimental manipulation of search using choice architecture.

Participants estimate current and historical racial economic equality (via questionnaire) relative to ground-truth statistics from national data. Outcomes measure estimation error (overestimate vs. underestimate) stratified by demographic group and belief factors. Analysis identifies systematic biases in estimation via comparison to national benchmarks and uses experimental manipulation of salience and anchoring to test causal inference mechanisms.
domain: social psychology, race perception, economic inequality

mechanism: Participants receive training in cognitive strategies to regulate craving (e.g., reappraisal) and are subsequently measured on food choice, craving rating, and consumption. Outcomes include preference shifts (willingness to pay), food selection, and caloric intake. Analysis estimates causal effects of cognitive-strategy training via randomized assignment and compares outcomes between trained and untrained conditions, assessing generalization to novel food items.
domain: health psychology, eating behavior, cognitive regulation

mechanism: Participants in a social network exchange beliefs about climate-data interpretation under bipartisan vs. partisan-prime conditions. Outcomes measure accuracy of climate-trend judgments and polarization metrics. Analysis tests whether exposure to opposing beliefs improves judgment accuracy by comparing accuracy across network-composition and priming conditions and identifies partisan-salience priming as a moderator that reduces social learning.
domain: collective intelligence, political psychology, climate communication
